aliases: Sliced pandan bread with sliced cheese

english: Sliced pandan bread with sliced cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pandan bread with cheese; mapped to the closest parent class 'bread food product' (FOODON_03315595). Pandan flavouring and cheese topping are not captured in this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000N (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for pandan-flavoured bread with processed cheese.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pandan bread with cheese as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of two slices of pandan bread (approximately 60–70 g) topped with one slice of processed cheese (approximately 15–20 g), totalling around 80 g.

nutrient_source.title: Pandan Bread with Cheese — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pandan Bread with Cheese (cr_id F05020073). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for processed cheese and enriched white bread components, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — White bread analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pandan bread with cheese in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is estimated by analogy to white bread (GI 75, glucose reference) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Pandan bread uses a refined wheat flour base similar to white bread; the addition of processed cheese slightly moderates the glycaemic response but the base GI is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 23.3) / 100 ≈ 17.5, based on an 80 g serving providing approximately 23.3 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 699 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold; processed cheese is the primary contributor
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit pandan bread with cheese due to its very high sodium content of 699 mg/100 g; if consumed, restrict to a single small slice and substitute processed cheese with a low-sodium natural cheese such as fresh mozzarella or ricotta.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (75) refined wheat flour base with 29.2 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 2.2 g dietary fibre/100 g; unfavourable carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit pandan bread with cheese due to its high glycaemic index of 75 and low dietary fibre content of 2.2 g/100 g; if consumed, choose a small portion, pair with a high-fibre salad, and request wholemeal pandan bread where available to reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (699 mg/100 g) and estimated saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g) from processed cheese contribute to cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of pandan bread with cheese given its high sodium content of 699 mg/100 g and saturated fat from processed cheese, both of which adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; substituting with a low-sodium natural cheese and limiting portion size is strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 699 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold; processed cheese contributes saturated fat and sodium; dietary fibre only 2.2 g/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid pandan bread with processed cheese due to its very high sodium content of 699 mg/100 g, which may worsen RA-related tissue swelling; the high calcium content of 910 mg/100 g is beneficial for patients on corticosteroids, so substituting with a low-sodium natural cheese is recommended to retain the calcium benefit while reducing sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pandan bread with cheese is a popular Singaporean breakfast and snack item consisting of sliced pandan-flavoured bread topped or sandwiched with processed cheese slices. Pandan bread is made from enriched wheat flour dough infused with pandan (screwpine) leaf extract or flavouring, which imparts a distinctive green colour and a mildly sweet, vanilla-like, grassy aroma. The bread is soft and pillowy in texture, similar to Asian-style milk bread, and is typically sold pre-sliced. Processed cheese slices — commonly a pasteurised cheese product — are layered on top or between slices, adding a creamy, salty, and mildly tangy flavour contrast to the sweet pandan base. This combination is widely available in Singaporean bakeries, kopitiams (traditional coffee shops), and supermarkets, and is commonly consumed at breakfast or as an afternoon snack. Nutritionally, the dish is notable for its high protein content (16.9 g/100 g), largely contributed by the processed cheese, and its very high calcium content (910 mg/100 g), which is beneficial for bone health. However, the sodium content is very high at 699 mg/100 g, primarily from the processed cheese, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The refined wheat flour base gives the bread a high glycaemic index (estimated GI 75), and the low dietary fibre content (2.2 g/100 g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without careful portion control. The dish is not vegan due to dairy content but is generally considered halal.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020073) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in an approved primary regional source. GI is estimated by analogy to white bread from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the closest defensible approved GI source; this introduces a minor uncertainty but does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong nutrient provenance.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for processed American cheese (USDA FDC ID 173414) and enriched white bread (USDA FDC ID 325871), weighted approximately 40% cheese and 60% bread by mass, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this specific composite pandan bread with cheese product are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (GI 75, glucose scale) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:12); no direct GI measurement for pandan bread with cheese exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load per serving calculated at 80 g serving size: (75 × 23.3 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 17.5. 4. Halal status is assumed based on typical commercial pandan bread and processed cheese formulations in Singapore; individual product verification is recommended. 5. The input record listed 'Embedded GI Database' as the GI source with confidence 0.312; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate per approved source policy.
